verses.text: After these things — the rescue, the meeting with Melchizedek, the refusal of Sodom's wealth — God comes to Abram in a vision: Fear not. I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward. The reward is God Himself.
Abram pushes back gently. Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless? His heir is Eliezer of Damascus, a household servant. The promise is starting to feel hypothetical.
Abram pushes back gently. Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless? His heir is Eliezer of Damascus, a household servant. The promise is starting to feel hypothetical.

God answers: this one shall not be your heir. He brings Abram outside and points to the night sky. Count the stars, if you can — so shall your offspring be. The promise gets bigger when faith asks honest questions.

God answers: this one shall not be your heir. He brings Abram outside and points to the night sky. Count the stars, if you can — so shall your offspring be. The promise gets bigger when faith asks honest questions.

And Abram believed the LORD; and He counted it to him for righteousness. This single sentence is later quoted by Paul and James as the doorway to understanding salvation by faith. Righteousness is not earned here — it is credited.
I am the LORD that brought you out of Ur. Abram still asks: how shall I know I will inherit this land? Faith and questions can coexist. God doesn't rebuke; He answers with a ceremony.
I am the LORD that brought you out of Ur. Abram still asks: how shall I know I will inherit this land? Faith and questions can coexist. God doesn't rebuke; He answers with a ceremony.

God names five animals: a heifer, a goat, a ram, a turtledove, a young pigeon — each three years old. Abram cuts them in half, lays them out, and drives away birds of prey. Ancient covenants were sealed by walking between cut animals.

God names five animals: a heifer, a goat, a ram, a turtledove, a young pigeon — each three years old. Abram cuts them in half, lays them out, and drives away birds of prey. Ancient covenants were sealed by walking between cut animals.

God names five animals: a heifer, a goat, a ram, a turtledove, a young pigeon — each three years old. Abram cuts them in half, lays them out, and drives away birds of prey. Ancient covenants were sealed by walking between cut animals.

As the sun goes down, a deep sleep falls on Abram, and a horror of great darkness. God tells him: your descendants will be strangers in a foreign land for 400 years; they will be enslaved and afflicted.

As the sun goes down, a deep sleep falls on Abram, and a horror of great darkness. God tells him: your descendants will be strangers in a foreign land for 400 years; they will be enslaved and afflicted.

But God will judge that nation, and Abram's offspring will come back with great wealth. The fourth generation will return — when the iniquity of the Amorites is full. God's timing is patient; even judgment waits.

But God will judge that nation, and Abram's offspring will come back with great wealth. The fourth generation will return — when the iniquity of the Amorites is full. God's timing is patient; even judgment waits.

But God will judge that nation, and Abram's offspring will come back with great wealth. The fourth generation will return — when the iniquity of the Amorites is full. God's timing is patient; even judgment waits.

After dark, a smoking furnace and a burning lamp pass between the pieces. Normally both parties of a covenant walk between the halves. Here only God walks. The covenant is one-sided — God binds Himself alone.
On that day God made a covenant with Abram, granting the land from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates. Ten nations are named who currently live there. The map is drawn before Abram has a child.
